blocks.context: For example, you can use `ref` to get a reference to the underlying DOM element of `Button` component:

blocks.code:
function App() {
-  return (
-    <Ref innerRef={buttonRef}>
-      <Button />
-    </Ref>
-  )
+  return <Button ref={buttonRef} />
}
blocks.context: Because of the native ref support, `Ref` component is no longer needed and was removed as it used deprecated APIs.

blocks.code:
-import { Ref } from "semantic-ui-react";
+import Ref from "@semantic-ui-react/component-ref";
blocks.context: As we exported `Ref` component and recommended its usage everywhere we moved out `Ref` component to a separate package (`@semantic-ui-react/component-ref`). This means that you can use it already with both v2 & v3 to make the upgrade smoother.

blocks.code:
-import { Visibility } from "semantic-ui-react";
+import Visibility from "@semantic-ui-react/component-visibility";
blocks.context: As it's not a straightforward replacement that may cause issues with adoption we moved out `Visibility` component to a separate package (`@semantic-ui-react/component-visibility`). This means that you can use it already with v2 or upcoming v3:

blocks.code:
-  static INITIAL = TRANSITION_STATUS_INITIAL
-  static ENTERED = TRANSITION_STATUS_ENTERED
-  static ENTERING = TRANSITION_STATUS_ENTERING
-  static EXITED = TRANSITION_STATUS_EXITED
-  static EXITING = TRANSITION_STATUS_EXITING
-  static UNMOUNTED = TRANSITION_STATUS_UNMOUNTED
blocks.context: Some static properties on `Transition` component were removed:

blocks.code:
<> - +
- + [-popper.width, 0]} />
blocks.context: Previously it was possible to pass different units to the offset prop as units. This behavior was changed and `offset` accepts a tuple or a function. Examples in our docs were also updated.

blocks.code:
-<Popup popperModifiers={{ preventOverflow: { padding: 0 } }} />
+<Popup popperModifiers={[{ name: 'preventOverflow', options: { padding: 0 } }]} />
blocks.context: The `popperModifiers` prop should be defined as an array with changed format (see [Popper docs](https://popper.js.org/docs/v2/migration-guide/#10-update-modifiers)).
[@artsy/fresnel](https://github.com/artsy/fresnel) was proposed as a replacement as it properly handles SSR scenarios.

blocks.code: <Button icon='like' />
blocks.context: Semantic UI React components can have "shorthands". For example, `Button` component has an `icon` shorthand which value defines the icon that will be rendered.

blocks.code:
<>
  {/* 💡 'color' and 'name' will be used as <Icon /> element's props */}
  <Button content='Like' icon={{ color: 'red', name: 'like' }} />
  {/* 💡 you can also add handlers and any DOM props to shorthands */}
  <Input
    action={{
      icon: 'search',
      onClick: () => console.log('An action was clicked!'),
    }}
    actionPosition='left'
    placeholder='Search...'
  />
</>
blocks.context: Each component's shorthand has an associated default element type. For example, by default, there is `<Icon />` element rendered for `Button`'s icon shorthand. It is possible to customize props of this default element by providing props object as shorthand value:

blocks.code:
<>
  <Button content='Like' icon='like' />
  {/* 💡 has identical effect to the previous one */}
  <Button content='Like' icon={{ name: 'like' }} />
</>
blocks.context: There is even shorter way to define default element's props - by using a primitive value. In that case provided shorthand value will be taken as a value for "default prop" of this element.

blocks.code:
<> Show} content='Content' /> {/* 💡 has identical effect to the previous one */} Show} content={{ content: 'Content' }} /> {/* ⛔ example below has broken styling, see section about React Element */} Show} content={
Content
} />
blocks.context: This works because `name` is the default prop of shorthand's `<Icon />` element.

blocks.code:
<>
  {/* 💡 hides a toogle icon in `Dropdown` */}
  <Dropdown icon={null} />
  <Dropdown icon={false} />
</>
blocks.context: It is also possible to pass falsy values (`false`, `null` or `undefined`) - in that case there will be nothing rendered for the component's shorthand.

blocks.code: <Button icon={<i className='my-icon' />} />
blocks.context: There are cases where it might be necessary to customize the element tree that will be rendered as a shorthand's value. Returning to `Button` example, we might want to render `<i />` instead of default `<Icon />` element. In that case, the necessary element might be directly provided as shorthand value:

blocks.code: <Button icon={{ as: 'i', className: 'my-icon' }} />
blocks.context: Due to this limitation, you should strive to use other options for shorthand values whenever is possible - for instance, this is how the previous example can be rewritten:

blocks.code:
<Button
  content='Like'
  icon={{
    children: (Component, componentProps) => <Component {...componentProps} color='red' />,
    name: 'question',
  }}
/>
blocks.context: Providing function as a shorthand value is the most involving but, at the same time, the most powerful option for customizing component's shorthand. It should return React Element as a result or `null`.

blocks.code:
<Button
  content='Like'
  icon={{ children: (Component, componentProps) => <Label basic>+1</Label> }}
/>
blocks.context: There is another use case when render function is very useful for - this is the case where custom element's tree should be rendered for the shorthand. As you might recall, there is a problem that might happen when React Element is provided directly as shorthand value - in that case, props are not propagated to rendered. In order to avoid that the following strategy should be considered:

blocks.code:
<Button
  content='Like'
  icon={(Component, componentProps) => <Component {...componentProps} color='red' name='like' />}
/>
blocks.context: Thus, in its simplest form, it could be used the following way:

blocks.code:
yarn add semantic-ui-react semantic-ui-css
## Or NPM
npm install semantic-ui-react semantic-ui-css
blocks.context: React components can be installed via yarn or npm:

blocks.code: import 'semantic-ui-css/semantic.min.css'
blocks.context: After install, **import the minified CSS file in your app's entry file:**

blocks.code:
<body>
  <!-- ... other HTML ... -->
  <!-- ... Load React ... -->

  <link
    async
    rel="stylesheet"
    href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/semantic-ui@2/dist/semantic.min.css"
  />
  <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/semantic-ui-react/dist/umd/semantic-ui-react.min.js"></script>

  <!-- Load our React component. -->
  <script src="like_button.js"></script>
</body>
blocks.context: And then just add this link and script tag to the `<body>` after initializing React in your `index.html` file.
</body>

blocks.code:
<>
  {/* 🔨  Each Semantic UI React component has a default value for `as` prop */}
  {/* Will output: <button class='ui button' /> */}
  <Button />
  {/* Uses another tag: <a class='ui button' /> */}
  <Button as='a' />
</>
blocks.context: Semantic UI React provides a way to compose React components through the `as` prop. It allows composing component features and props without adding extra nested components.

Our components handle only props that are defined in their `propTypes`, all unhandled props are passed to the component you are rendering `as`. It means that all HTML props are supported on all components.

blocks.code:
import { Link } from 'react-router-dom'
import { Button } from 'semantic-ui-react'

// 💡 `to` prop is not handled in `Button` and will be passed to `Link` component
;<Button as={Link} to='/home'>
  To homepage
</Button>
blocks.context: This is also essential for composing with third party libraries like `react-router`.

blocks.code: npm uninstall semantic-ui semantic-ui-css
blocks.context: First of all, please remove existing references for `semantic-ui` & `semantic-ui-css` package or styles included from CDN to avoid styles duplication.

blocks.code: yarn remove semantic-ui semantic-ui-css
blocks.context: npm uninstall semantic-ui semantic-ui-css

blocks.code:
npm install @craco/craco @semantic-ui-react/craco-less --save-dev
npm install semantic-ui-less --save
blocks.context: Create React App does not support LESS out of the box; we propose to use [`@craco/craco`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@craco/craco) to add its support and avoid `eject`. You have to install required packages:

blocks.code:
yarn add @craco/craco @semantic-ui-react/craco-less --dev
yarn add semantic-ui-less
blocks.context: npm install semantic-ui-less --save
And then update your `package.json` and create `craco.config.js`:

blocks.code: npx @semantic-ui-react/bootstrap
blocks.context: We made a special CLI tool that will copy necessary files with the skeleton of your customization.
This file is mostly prepared, but you have to change some details in the bottom of it:

blocks.code: import 'semantic-ui-less/semantic.less'
blocks.context: You have to import `semantic.less` file in your entry file.

blocks.code: yarn start

blocks.context: Start by [forking Semantic UI React][12] to your GitHub account.  Then clone your fork and install dependencies:

blocks.code: git remote add upstream git@github.com:Semantic-Org/Semantic-UI-React.git
blocks.context: Add our repo as a git remote so you can pull/rebase your fork with our latest updates:

blocks.code:
yarn start                 // run doc site

yarn ci                    // run all checks CI runs

yarn test                  // test once
yarn test:watch            // test on file change

yarn build                 // build everything
yarn build:dist            // build dist
yarn build:docs            // build docs
yarn build:docs-toc        // build toc for markdown files

yarn deploy:docs           // deploy gh-pages doc site

yarn lint                  // lint once
yarn lint:fix              // lint and attempt to fix
yarn lint:watch            // lint on file change

Stateless components should be written as a `function`:
Stateful components should be classes:
Every component must have fully described `propTypes`.

blocks.code:
<Segment basic />                     // standalone
<Segment floated='left' />            // pairs
<Segment padded />                    // mixed
<Segment padded='very' />
<Segment size='small' color='red' />  // groups
blocks.context: #### API Patterns
<Segment size='small' color='red' />  // groups

blocks.code:
import cx from 'clsx'
import { useKeyOnly, useValueAndKey, useKeyOrValueAndKey } from '../../lib'

function Segment({ size, color, basic, floated, padded }) {
  const classes = cx(
    'ui',
    size,
    color,
    useKeyOnly(basic, 'basic'),
    useValueAndKey(floated, 'floated'),
    useKeyOrValueAndKey(padded, 'padded'),
    'segment'
  )

  return <div className={classes} />
}
blocks.context: Use [`classNameBuilders`][4] to extract the prop values and build up the `className`.  Grouped classes like `color` and `size` simply use the prop value as the `className`.

blocks.code:
import * as common from 'test/specs/commonTests'
import Segment from 'src/elements/Segment/Segment'

describe('Segment', () => {
  common.propValueOnlyToClassName(Segment, 'size')
  common.propValueOnlyToClassName(Segment, 'color')
  common.propKeyOnlyToClassName(Segment, 'basic')
  common.propKeyAndValueToClassName(Segment, 'floated')
  common.propKeyOrValueAndKeyToClassName(Segment, 'padded')
})
blocks.context: Use [`commonTests`](#common-tests) to test the `className` build up for each prop.  These tests will run your component through all the possible usage permutations:

blocks.context: Sometimes it is convenient to use props to generate markup.  Example, the [Label][10] markup is minimal.  One configuration includes an image and detail:

blocks.code:
<Label
  image='veronika.jpg'
  detail='Friend'
  text='Veronica'
/>
blocks.context: We allow props to define these minimal *component parts*:

blocks.code:
common.isConformant()
common.hasUIClassName()
common.hasSubcomponents()
common.isTabbable()
common.rendersChildren()

common.implementsIconProp()
common.implementsImageProp()
common.implementsTextAlignProp()
common.implementsVerticalAlignProp()
common.implementsWidthProp()

common.propKeyOnlyToClassName()

blocks.code:
import React from 'react'
import * as common from 'test/specs/commonTests'
import Menu from 'src/collections/Menu/Menu'
import MenuItem from 'src/collections/Menu/MenuItem'

describe('Menu', () => {
  common.isConformant(Menu)
  common.hasUIClassName(Menu)
  common.rendersChildren(Menu)
  common.hasSubcomponents(Menu, [MenuItem]) // some take additional arguments
})
blocks.context: Every common test receives your component as its first argument.
The last argument to a common test is always `options`.  You can configure the test here. For example, if your component requires certain props to render, you can pass in `requiredProps`:
Strive to use stateless functional components when possible:
If your component requires event handlers, it is a stateful class component. Want to know [why][15]?
